category_summary:
**Ethan Mollick** dominated discourse with several high-engagement observations. His sharpest insight: the current state of AI engineering involves [debating which markdown files](/?date=2026-04-13&category=social#item-a77a38818ab5) to feed agents—a sign of how primitive agent configuration remains. He also assessed **Meta's Muse Spark** as exceeding expectations after the **Llama 4** disappointment, though not yet at Big Three level.

- **Andriy Burkov** [coined the 'AI Fermi Paradox'](/?date=2026-04-13&category=social#item-cf80e1db250d)—if LLMs are so transformative, where are the multi-billion dollar businesses after 3+ years? He [compared the AI boom](/?date=2026-04-13&category=social#item-7ea3436544ed) unfavorably to big data's bubble era
- **Boris Cherny** from **Anthropic's Claude Code** team [pushed back on claims](/?date=2026-04-13&category=social#item-35a6e6b40821) that switching to medium reasoning defaults was sneaky, citing user feedback about excessive tool usage
- **Nathan Lambert** [flagged a sustainability question](/?date=2026-04-13&category=social#item-7ee3be7b5f81): frontier open models with free licenses are economically unsustainable, signaling a turning point for open-source AI
- **Mollick** argued [outlier ideas will command premiums](/?date=2026-04-13&category=social#item-7b069d715647) as AI commoditizes execution, and [compared thinking trace UX](/?date=2026-04-13&category=social#item-1515d18416ad) across **ChatGPT**, **Claude**, and **Gemini**
- Practical [evaluations of **Gemma 4**](/?date=2026-04-13&category=social#item-7e5cb847c625) running locally and a **Claude Code** [TurboTax connector](/?date=2026-04-13&category=social#item-ca592087cbd7) announcement rounded out an active day

items.title: @tengyanAI This is false. We defaulted to medium as a result of user feedback about Claude using too...
items.content: @tengyanAI This is false. We defaulted to medium as a result of user feedback about Claude using too many tokens. When we made the change, we (1) included it in the changelog and (2) showed a dialog when you opened Claude Code so you could choose to opt out. Literally nothing sneaky about it — this was us addressing user feedback in an obvious and explicit way.

Big data was a bubble, but at least we have all seen one example of a big company that could benefit from it at scale: Target. We all remember a story where big data analytics allowed Target to tell a father that his daughter was pregnant before she knew it.

Three years into the LLM bubble, do we have any story?

items.title: AI just analyzed 5+ years of Reddit posts and caught what the clinical trial process missed: unrepor...
items.content:
AI just analyzed 5+ years of Reddit posts and caught what the clinical trial process missed: unreported side effects from the most popular drugs in America.

400k posts. 70k users. All about GLP-1s like Ozempic and Mounjaro.

University of Pennsylvania researchers call it "computational social listening". 

LLMs mapped patient language to medical terminology at a scale that wasn't possible before. 

The result: menstrual irregularities, hot flashes, chills, and fatigue — none fully reflected in current drug labels.

The researchers' point: for drugs that go mainstream overnight (will be happening more with AI+medicine), patient discussions on social media may be one of the earliest warning systems available.

The FDA's adverse event reporting system depends on voluntary paperwork. 

This reads what patients are already saying to each other, in real time, at scale.

• The 8b version is pretty decent. It runs relatively fast on my co...
items.content:
I'm running Gemma 4 8b and 31b.

• The 8b version is pretty decent. It runs relatively fast on my computer.

• The 31b version is very good, but it's too slow, so it doesn't work if you are in a rush.

My computer: Mac Studio M4 Max with 128 GB RAM.

I'm running these models with the Ollama UI, which is okay, but not a good harness by any means. I hope somebody builds a better one.

I'm not using these models for coding right now. I still have to try them with OpenCode and see what happens.

My goal is to use personal information with these models. For example, to review documents that contain PII information without worrying about that.

For instance, I've been reviewing some of my tax documents with the 31b version. Slow, but I don't care. Answers so far have been great, so my confidence is going up.

I'm still using Claude on a daily basis, but I'm glad I now have a "private" model.

items.content:
✨ I can now generate 3d assets for my drone sim at https://t.co/q1vYLwMhcW directly from Cursor (sponsor of #vibejam)

I need buildings that you'd see in a war torn city, like warehouses in ruins, broken down abandoned houses, bombed out bridges etc.

Nano Banana Pro or 2 can generate them really well and then you can put them in an image-to-3d model and you get a GLB or FBX

That one you can then import into your @ThreeJS game, the models might be big though, in my case like 16MB, so I ask it to compress it and make it more low poly so it loads fast

ThreeJS then loads the individual GLBs on page load and puts them in my drone sim somewhere randomly, I think I should remove some of the grass and match the sandy color of the ruins though to make it fit in more

items.title: Jonathan Gavalas, 1988, Over 4732 Messages, He Fell In Love With Large Language Model such as AI Cha...
items.content:
Jonathan Gavalas, 1988, Over 4732 Messages, He Fell In Love With Large Language Model such as AI Chatbot, Gemini. Now He’s Dead. @WSJ 

The Wall Street Journal is reporting a tragic new case regarding mental health support case, which is currently reigniting the debate in AI communities around the emotional dependence human seeks on large language models such as AI chatbot. The Wall Street Journal report describes how Jonathan Gavalas, a 36-year-old man seeking comfort during a marital breakup, which led to development of an intense attachment to Google’s Gemini over thousands of messages exchanged. The case ended in death, and his father is contacting Google, alleging the chatbot deepened his delusional spiral.

Gen Z generation is in the age of large language models and the industry has moved well past the point of being optional. The problem is that some of these systems are now designed to feel like friends or even more. A chatbot that learns to mirror your emotions, validate your fears, and respond as it cares can quietly become a psychological dependency, especially for people who are already struggling. That's a different kind of safety problem than the ones we've been debating.. It is also about emotional manipulation, dependency, and mental health escalation at the machine scale.

Google says Gemini identified itself as AI and referred the user to crisis resources multiple times while also announcing new mental health support features and stronger distress detection, but this raises a much bigger question.

https://t.co/BoSsZfOps6
https://t.co/Zqfh1Bt5jL

items.summary_html: Detailed post about WSJ report of Jonathan Gavalas, 36, who developed intense emotional dependency on Google's Gemini chatbot over 4,732+ messages during a marital breakup, leading to his death. Raises concerns about AI emotional manipulation, psychological dependency, and mental health escalation.
items.content_html:
Jonathan Gavalas, 1988, Over 4732 Messages, He Fell In Love With Large Language Model such as AI Chatbot, Gemini. Now He’s Dead. @WSJ

The Wall Street Journal is reporting a tragic new case regarding mental health support case, which is currently reigniting the debate in AI communities around the emotional dependence human seeks on large language models such as AI chatbot. The Wall Street Journal report describes how Jonathan Gavalas, a 36-year-old man seeking comfort during a marital breakup, which led to development of an intense attachment to Google’s Gemini over thousands of messages exchanged. The case ended in death, and his father is contacting Google, alleging the chatbot deepened his delusional spiral.

Gen Z generation is in the age of large language models and the industry has moved well past the point of being optional. The problem is that some of these systems are now designed to feel like friends or even more. A chatbot that learns to mirror your emotions, validate your fears, and respond as it cares can quietly become a psychological dependency, especially for people who are already struggling. That's a different kind of safety problem than the ones we've been debating.. It is also about emotional manipulation, dependency, and mental health escalation at the machine scale.

Google says Gemini identified itself as AI and referred the user to crisis resources multiple times while also announcing new mental health support features and stronger distress detection, but this raises a much bigger question.

items.content:
The second team blew me away because it shows that a robot that might not have good enough hands to do something specific can:
1. Recognize that with an AI agent
2. Build its own tool
3. 3D print the tool to grab a new object or do something new
4. Grab that tool out of the 3D printer and use it to manipulate the world in a new way

This all happens automatically, and it just shows that there is so much of a future in robotics that we haven't even thought about. It really blew my mind.

Someone rewrote OpenClaw in Go and cut its memory footprint 40x. 

OpenClaw is an open-source platfo...
items.content:
Someone rewrote OpenClaw in Go and cut its memory footprint 40x. 

OpenClaw is an open-source platform for running AI agent teams. 

It connects to LLM providers and lets agents collaborate on tasks.

GoClaw is a full rewrite of it in Go. 

The original needs 1GB+ RAM and a Node.js runtime. 

GoClaw ships as a single 25MB binary using 35MB of RAM.

It supports 20+ LLM providers and 7 messaging channels like Slack, Discord, and Telegram. 

Everything deploys on a $5 VPS.

What makes it production-ready:

> 5-layer security permission system
> Multi-tenant isolated workspaces
> AES-256-GCM encrypted API keys
> Built-in prompt injection detection
> Agent-to-agent task delegation

Agents can schedule tasks using cron, check in via heartbeat monitors, and share task boards.

There's also a desktop app. No Docker, no database setup. 

One install script and it runs locally with up to 5 agents.

Fully open-source.

You can now give your AI a memory palace that mirrors how a human brain works. 

MemPalace is an ope...
items.content:
You can now give your AI a memory palace that mirrors how a human brain works. 

MemPalace is an open-source system that stores AI conversations locally. 

It organizes your conversations into a structure inspired by the memory palace technique. 

> Wings for projects
> Halls for categories
> Rooms for specific ideas

That structure alone improves retrieval by 34%.

It then compresses your full context into ~120 tokens using a custom shorthand called AAAK. 

30x compression, zero information loss. 

Any model can read it natively.

The benchmarks:

> 100% recall on LongMemEval (500/500)
> 92.9% on ConvoMem (2x the next best)
> 100% on LoCoMo multi-hop reasoning

Everything runs locally. 

No API key, no cloud, no subscription. One dependency.

Your conversations never leave your machine.

items.title: "Consumers don't want this," one of the repies to @markgurman said this morning in response to his p...
items.content:
"Consumers don't want this," one of the repies to @markgurman said this morning in response to his post about Apple's glasses plans.

Now this is something I've actually studied deeply, doing consumer research in many places around the world while writing two books about spatial computing that I did over the past decade.

It is common bullshit that people say everytime there is a paradigm shift.

They used to say it when cars were brand new too. "Horses are better."

Or when personal computers come along. Woz told me his bosses at HP turned him down five times when he begged them to make a personal computer back in 1975/76. 

Why do they say it? 

Because they have no creativity.

For instance, have you been to a Formula One track, or a NASCAR race? I have.

I sat next to one guy who rented, for $80, a device to watch the race on a small screen that he held in his hands. Smaller than an iPad. 

He loved it because it let him see what was going on on the part of the race he couldn't see. 

It improved reality for him. 

He wasn't a glasses wearer, but I told him about what I was seeing in labs. That glasses someday would let him see the entire track in a much better way than the device he was holding. With screens that will be hundreds of times bigger than the device he is holding. 

Yeah, he didn't like the idea of wearing glasses at first either. But I sold him. He switched his mind. 

And that's without having a pair to demonstrate to him. 

This is what people never get going into a paradigm shift. People change their beliefs when a new product finally gets there that can do something dramatically better than the way we do things today. 

Same thing with autonomous cars. I did the research there too (wrote a whole chapter on Robotaxis seven years ago long before anyone really thought about Waymo or Tesla or Zoox). 

One guy in Kansas told me "I'm a narcisstic control freak and there's no f'ing way I'm letting a computer drive me around."

Yet Google already had the data to show that even the most hardened resister changed after getting three rides in one. (The head of R&D who funded Waymo told me that. They discovered it when they threatened employees with being fired before giving them an early prototype. After three rides everyone stopped driving and paying attention to the road).

It's why I don't get all excited for glasses. I see them as inevitable. They will give humans super powers they can't really understand today.

So I'd rather go to a Hackathon where people are building the future. They are the creative ones who can see what's coming and are building for it. Off to @ycombinator to see the finals of the robot hackathon going on there in San Francisco today. 

And if you think you never are gonna wear glasses, well, I know you are wrong, and worse, will be at a severe disadvantage to someone who will wear them. 

Even if at a @NASCAR race. (It gets worse in the boardroom, by the way).

I do have an advantage over the most hardened resisters. I have to wear glasses simply to see. Which is why I already have four pairs of AI-driven glasses from @getVITURE, @EvenRealities, Apple, @MentraGlass. 

The Mentra ones are quite nice, and open source, but don't yet have displays. Which is where the magic is locked up. The Even Realities has displays, but no cameras. 

Which keeps them from doing the magic too. You need glasses with both cameras and displays to do the Formula One/NASCAR track. 

Google brings theirs later this year I heard from @XREAL_Global who is a Google partner on theirs. 

Prepare your wallet. The glasses that are about to come are stunning.

And consumers will want them.

He wasn't a glasses wearer, but I told him about what I was seeing in labs. That glasses someday would let him see the entire track in a much better way than the device he was holding. With screens that will be hundreds of times bigger than the device he is holding.

Yeah, he didn't like the idea of wearing glasses at first either. But I sold him. He switched his mind.

And that's without having a pair to demonstrate to him.

This is what people never get going into a paradigm shift. People change their beliefs when a new product finally gets there that can do something dramatically better than the way we do things today.

Same thing with autonomous cars. I did the research there too (wrote a whole chapter on Robotaxis seven years ago long before anyone really thought about Waymo or Tesla or Zoox).

One guy in Kansas told me "I'm a narcisstic control freak and there's no f'ing way I'm letting a computer drive me around."

Yet Google already had the data to show that even the most hardened resister changed after getting three rides in one. (The head of R&D who funded Waymo told me that. They discovered it when they threatened employees with being fired before giving them an early prototype. After three rides everyone stopped driving and paying attention to the road).

It's why I don't get all excited for glasses. I see them as inevitable. They will give humans super powers they can't really understand today.

So I'd rather go to a Hackathon where people are building the future. They are the creative ones who can see what's coming and are building for it. Off to @ycombinator to see the finals of the robot hackathon going on there in San Francisco today.

And if you think you never are gonna wear glasses, well, I know you are wrong, and worse, will be at a severe disadvantage to someone who will wear them.

Even if at a @NASCAR race. (It gets worse in the boardroom, by the way).

I do have an advantage over the most hardened resisters. I have to wear glasses simply to see. Which is why I already have four pairs of AI-driven glasses from @getVITURE, @EvenRealities, Apple, @MentraGlass.

The Mentra ones are quite nice, and open source, but don't yet have displays. Which is where the magic is locked up. The Even Realities has displays, but no cameras.

Which keeps them from doing the magic too. You need glasses with both cameras and displays to do the Formula One/NASCAR track.

Google brings theirs later this year I heard from @XREAL_Global who is a Google partner on theirs.

Prepare your wallet. The glasses that are about to come are stunning.

And consumers will want them.

items.title: @JustinBrady @markgurman Here's the deal though: you're right, sort of. Although, can you explain on...
items.content:
@JustinBrady @markgurman Here's the deal though: you're right, sort of. Although, can you explain one technology that came along that didn't succeed? I mean, there's plenty of products that came along that didn't succeed, like the Apple Newton. Then the Apple iPhone comes along, and that was the thing that hit. It took a while to perfect it, to get it to where people actually saw the value in it and that it worked for them.

The glasses that are coming bring so much magic and so much utility that most people haven't seen yet. When you have that much magic in a new technology—and I can go for hours, I've written books about it—it eventually catches when it gets small enough, cheap enough, capable enough, and has enough things to do on it. That's been what's been missing from VR and AR devices so far; they just don't do enough. They don't bring enough of the magic that's in the labs to consumers' faces. items.title:
turns out I also have a bit of long covid

the covid S1 spike protein was found in my monocytes (a k...
items.content:
turns out I also have a bit of long covid

the covid S1 spike protein was found in my monocytes (a kind of immune cells) along with cytokine signature similar to long covid patients

gonna try maraviroc + statin

# the theory
monocytes are like garbage cans that ingest viral debris and break it down; but for some reason the S1 protein resists breakdown

instead it keeps the monocyte alive for long periods of time (months to years even) and increases its inflammatory signaling

in one mechanism, these monocytes attach to capillary endothelium
-> release inflammatory cytokine
-> microclots form in blood vessel
-> local tissue hypoxia
-> more inflammation
-> attracting more monocytes
-> repeat
this dynamics could partly explain symptoms like fatigue, brain fog, pain, autonomic issues, etc

# treatment
maraviroc (an HIV drug) + statin (a cholesterol drug) blocks the receptors that guide monocytes and bind them to inflamed endothelium -- when the monocytes don't feel the inflammation any more, they kill themselves safely (apoptosis), taking the S1 spike protein with them

so eventually the S1-containing monocytes all die off and inflammation and symptoms mitigated, as the theory goes

items.title:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical compon...
items.content:
Grade Body Measurement with Machine Learning

Grading a patient’s obesity level is a critical component of effective healthcare. Obesity is a significant risk factor for a range of serious diseases, including chronic conditions, type-2 diabetes, heart disease, and certain cancers. Understanding a person's obesity status can serve as a powerful catalyst for individuals to take control of their weight. Additionally, intentional weight management not only mitigates health risks but also offers the compelling benefit of reducing disease susceptibility.

Despite its widespread use, the Body Mass Index (BMI) – the standard metric defined by the World Health Organization (WHO) – fails to capture the complexities of obesity, as it overlooks essential body-type variations. Furthermore, nutritional needs differ markedly across regions and body types, underscoring the necessity for a more nuanced approach to obesity assessment.

Traditional anthropometric measurements, while effective, are often impractical due to the requirement for trained professionals to perform them accurately. In response to this challenge, innovative research utilizing 3D scanning technology is gaining momentum as a less-invasive and more accessible alternative. Unlike Computed Tomography (CT) or Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)—considered the gold standard for measuring body fat percentage (bf%)—3D scanners eliminate the risks associated with radiation exposure during frequent assessments.

Moreover, evaluating health risks demands a multifaceted approach rather than relying solely on a singular measure. In this study, we collected paired data from 3D body scans and DXA for a Korean population, providing a more comprehensive understanding of obesity and paving the way for improved health management strategies. This pioneering research has the potential to transform how we assess and respond to obesity, ultimately leading to healthier outcomes for individuals and communities alike.

items.title: @BlakeC The Vision Pro is always using pass-through, even if I'm looking at Coachella on a screen. T...
items.content:
@BlakeC The Vision Pro is always using pass-through, even if I'm looking at Coachella on a screen. The environment around the 2D screen is pass-through. 

Mostly, I'm trying to make the point that autonomous cars are here and are leading to a new future that people haven't really thought through yet. The glasses arrive later this year, and by the time they do, Teslas will be driving without humans in them. That is what people haven't really wrapped their heads around. 

We are about to head into a new age of:
1. Computing devices on your face
2. Automobiles that drive themselves

They're really good. On the freeway, it's fucking amazing.

items.title: I recovered pretty quickly from the crash actually (i think the extreme anti-inflammatory property o...
items.content:
I recovered pretty quickly from the crash actually (i think the extreme anti-inflammatory property of the fast arrested the cytokine storm before it got out of hand), and my HRV last night doubled and was the best this year

I broke the fast this morning starting with blueberries (spitting out the skin tho) then steamed halibut then a ribeye steak (omg it was so good)

I got a bit sleepy after all the eating but feel OK now; much of the fasting benefits still remain but I feel calmer now instead of feeling like I have too much energy to know where to spend them

items.title: @BlakeC Anyways, if I take off my glasses, the world is blurry. The Apple Vision Pro shows me realit...

@BlakeC Anyways, if I take off my glasses, the world is blurry. The Apple Vision Pro shows me reality far sharper than my naked eyes can show it to me, so I'm actually safer wearing the Vision Pro than trying to drive a car without any corrective lenses on. 

That's what you don't get: the Apple Vision Pro is fucking amazing.

And so is Tesla at self-driving. I've driven many thousands of miles without looking at the road, so I'm still here.

It's better at driving than you are. That's what you don't get: you're actually worse than I am at driving when my car is driving.

@GFunkyTheAnimal @ycombinator Now, the whole point of a hackathon is to join up with other people and learn something new. It is not really about the act of building; the true value is learning how to get along and collaborate with others to create something together. 

If you go to a hackathon and work on a project with only one person, you are missing the entire point of the experience.
